content: Hi! I'm enjoying the chance to participate in the MIT categories seminar here. Does anyone know of similar now-online seminars/chats for branches of math other than category theory?
@Marc Gotliboym are you on the AlgTop listserv by any chance? I know a few things have been posted there
@Jonathan Beardsley Nope, I'm not. Where could I find that?

so the listserv is here: https://lists.lehigh.edu/mailman/listinfo/algtop-l

but that my not help you now, since as far as I can tell, you can't access logs of it. I know Dev Sinha is running an algebraic topology thing out of MIT that will be available online. I'm not able at the moment to hunt down the links though
Got it, thanks :) I'll keep an eye out for that algebraic topology one
Looks like you can e-mail Dev, at dps@uoregon.edu, to get an invite to his MIT algebraic topology seminar. Here's the topics/info:

A description of the perspective and topics are below. The lectures will be at noon, Eastern Time, on Mondays starting on the 30th. For those who haven’t attended so far there will be a one-time optional “catch up” lecture at 11am Eastern Time on the 30th. Folks can also take a look at the accessible expository paper https://arxiv.org/abs/math/0610236, which was the topic of lecture 2.

Algebraic Topology from a Geometric Viewpoint
Mondays at noon, re-starting March 30. (With one additional cial “catch up” lecture March 30 at 11)

The level will be aimed at graduate students, not only in algebraic topology but in geometric topology as well as algebraic or differential or symplectic geometry, as well as anyone else interested in a geometric take on “intermediate” topics in algebraic topology. I will only assume basic algebraic topology (a year long course) and some basic differential topology (a subset of what is in the book by Guillemin-Pollack).

The topics I plan to treat in such a manner are as follows.
(Catch-up lecture) Homology and cohomology of configurations in Euclidean space - these give rise to some fun combinatorics, and govern the homology of spaces of maps from spheres.
Homotopy periods / Hopf invariants - up to torsion all maps from spheres to a simply connected space are determined by “higher linking invariants, with correction.” The representatives in de Rham theory agree with integrals arising in field theory. This also gives rise to a new approach to the lower central series of groups, currently being written up (which has planned applications ranging from non-simply connected rational homotopy theory to the Johnson filtration).
Homology and cohomology of infinite Grassmannians - revisiting this standard calculation.
The geometry of Eilenberg-MacLane spaces, and their homology and cohomology.
Homology and cohomology of unordered configuration spaces - in particular, cohomology of symmetric groups.
The Blakers-Massey Theorem, and its application to configuration space models for spaces of embeddings, and resulting knot and link invariants.
Geometry of Steenrod operations, and progress towards E_\infty models for cochains on manifolds through intersection and linking.

Oh, great, thanks, this looks like exactly the sort of thing I'm looking for.

I found that the listserv does also have an archive available conveniently https://lists.lehigh.edu/pipermail/algtop-l/
Oh wonderful!
Also, there's the MathOverflow Homotopy Theory Chatroom. To talk in there you have to have some small amount of "reputation" on MathOverflow, but I don't think it's a lot?
https://chat.stackexchange.com/rooms/9417/homotopy-theory
ooh, neat :)
Also I just got an email about this probability seminar: https://www.wim.uni-mannheim.de/doering/one-world/
Thanks! I need to find a way to dip my toes in probability
https://sites.google.com/view/nialltaggartmath/oats
https://s.wayne.edu/isaksen/echt/

Oooh thanks. That page also links to this collection of other online seminars:
https://s.wayne.edu/isaksen/online-mathematics-seminars/
there is a matrix/IRC room for algebraic topology, but it's basically empty (alas)
and there's the HoTT IRC channel, but i'm not sure if that's now defunct, given the existence of this zulip
Oh, there's now a discord for an online AT seminar, but it's still mostly un-alive https://discord.gg/Fze2D9

and there's the HoTT IRC channel, but i'm not sure if that's now defunct, given the existence of this zulip

there's a HoTT Zulip too, which seems to be somewhat active: https://hott.zulipchat.com/ (no invitation required)
